articles.title: Before the Internet: How the Declaration of Independence Became Breaking News in 1776
articles.summary: No internet. No TV. No phones. So how did people in the Thirteen Colonies learn about the Declaration of Independence? A new book, 'When the Declaration of Independence Was News' by historian Emily Sneff, reveals the fascinating story. The news spread through printed broadsides (poster-size sheets) by printer John Dunlap, newspaper reprints, and public readings at courthouses and churches. By the time the famous signed parchment was ready in August 1776, most people already knew. Reactions ranged from joyful celebrations to private worries. Printer Mary Katharine Goddard, the only woman printing under her own name, later produced the first copies with signers' names.

articles.title: Injuries Sideline Alcaraz and Djokovic from Madrid Open, French Open Hopes in Doubt
articles.summary: Top tennis stars Carlos Alcaraz (world No. 2) and Novak Djokovic (world No. 4) have withdrawn from the Madrid Open due to injuries. Alcaraz, 22, is struggling with a wrist problem that also forced him out of the Barcelona Open. He called missing his home tournament 'incredibly difficult.' Djokovic, 38, hasn't played since March because of a shoulder injury and skipped several Masters events. Both are racing to be fit for the French Open (May 18–June 7). Alcaraz is the two-time defending champion there. British player Jack Draper also retired from his Barcelona match with a knee injury.

articles.title: Descend Into a Volcano: Iceland's Thríhnúkagígur Offers a Journey to an Empty Magma Chamber
articles.summary: Thríhnúkagígur, a dormant volcano near Reykjavík, Iceland, is the only place on Earth where people can descend into an empty magma chamber. The chamber, left hollow after an eruption 4,500 years ago, is 700 feet deep — more than double the height of the Statue of Liberty. Visitors take an open cable elevator down to a 33,600-square-foot cave floor with colorful walls (bronze, indigo, yellow, orange). Scientists aren't sure why the magma disappeared; it may have drained back into the crust. The colors are from microbes and sulfur gases. Tours have been running since 2012.
